coredata.dc:title: Mg2+-doped carbon dots synthesized based on Lycium ruthenicum in cell imaging and promoting osteogenic differentiation in vitro
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Zhang, Ran
Cheng, Huaiyi
Wang, Yue
Zhang, Qingmei
Zhang, Lupeng
Wang, Lu
Li, Ran
Wu, Xiuping
Li, Bing
coredata.dc:description: The construction of biomaterials suitable for bone regeneration has been a serious challenge in the medical field. Considering that the process of bone regeneration is difficult to monitor, the application of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) materials for bone regeneration has received much attention. Here, we developed a Mg2+-doped biogenic carbon dot material and characterized it to determine the optimal ratio of carbon and magnesium sources in the synthesis of the material. Subsequently, its biocompatibility with MC3T3-E1 Subclone 4 (MC3T3) was investigated, and its high biocompatibility was supported by the preparation of biocarbon dots using natural lycium ruthenicum as the carbon source. In addition, our experimental results showed that the appropriate concentration of Mg-CDs was suitable for long-term cell growth and the cells had better growth, proliferation, and osteogenic differentiation ability. This Mg2+-doped biomass carbon dot shows great potential in bone tissue regeneration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Magnesium
Osteogenic differentiation
Biocompatibility

Biocompatibility and cytofluorimetric imaging of Mg-CDs
Real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR for osteogenic gene expression
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and Mg-CDs
Effect of Mg-CDs on the proliferation of MC3T3
Effect of Mg-CDs on osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of CDs and Mg-CDs; Observed under UV excitation and visible light. UV–vis absorption, maximum PL excitation, andemission spectra of CDs (b) and Mg-CDs (d); PL spectra of CDs(c) and Mg-CDs (e) under different wavelengths excitation.
(a) XPS analysis of Mg-CDs in full spectra. High resolution (b)C1s, (c)N1s, (d)O1s and (e)Mg 1 s peaks of CDs. High resolution (f)C1s, (g)N1s, (h)O1s and (i) Mg 1 s peaks of Mg-CDs.
EDS of CDs and Mg- CDs (The ratio of C and Mg is the mass ratio of the preparation materials.).
TEM images and size distribution histogram of (a)Mg-CDs and(b) Mg-CDs. (c) HRTEM images of Mg-CDs.(d)XRD pattern of CDs and Mg-CDs.(e)Zeta Potential of Mg-CDs.
In vitro cell proliferation at 1 day in culture conditions of Mg-CDs as determined by MTT.
Cell imaging at 40x magnification under 200 µg mL−1 Mg-CDs culture conditions (a)405 nm excitation, (b)488 nm excitation, (c)561 nm excitation, (d) Brightfield.

(a) Effect of Mg-CDs on the relative ALP activity of MC3T3 at different incubation concentrations and incubation times. (b) Relative ALP activity of control, CDs, and Mg-CDs groups at 3 and 7 days under 200 µg mL−1 treatment. One-way ANOVA was used for the statistical analysis, and Tukey's multiple comparison test came next. Data represent mean ± standard deviation, n = 3, * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, ** P < 0.001, **** P < 0.0001.
Mg-CDs stimulate calcium deposition in MC3T3. (a) Control, (b) Mg-CDs concentration of 12.5 µg mL−1, (c)25 µg mL−1, (d)50 μg/mL, (e)100 µg mL−1, (f)200 µg mL−1.

Osteogenesis-related gene expression of ALP (A), Runx-2 (B), Col-I (C) and OC (D) mRNAs in osteogenically induced MC3T3 at days 7 and 14 under CDs and Mg-CDs culture conditions. One-way ANOVA was used for the statistical analysis, and Tukey's multiple comparison test came next. Data represent mean ± standard deviation, n = 3, * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001, **** P < 0.0001.
Sequences of primers for real-time PCR.
Elemental content of materials prepared with different ratios of carbon and magnesium sources.

Lattice spacing: 0.21 nm

Average diameter: 2.0 nm
Lattice spacing: 0.2 nm
Promote osteogenic differentiation (increased ALP activity, alizarin red staining and upregulation of osteogenic-related mRNA expression)

head.ce:title: Mg2+-doped carbon dots synthesized based on Lycium ruthenicum in cell imaging and promoting osteogenic differentiation in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials, Taiyuan 030001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School and Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of biomaterials suitable for bone regeneration has been a serious challenge in the medical field. Considering that the process of bone regeneration is difficult to monitor, the application of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) materials for bone regeneration has received much attention. Here, we developed a Mg2+-doped biogenic carbon dot material and characterized it to determine the optimal ratio of carbon and magnesium sources in the synthesis of the material. Subsequently, its biocompatibility with MC3T3-E1 Subclone 4 (MC3T3) was investigated, and its high biocompatibility was supported by the preparation of biocarbon dots using natural lycium ruthenicum as the carbon source. In addition, our experimental results showed that the appropriate concentration of Mg-CDs was suitable for long-term cell growth and the cells had better growth, proliferation, and osteogenic differentiation ability. This Mg2+-doped biomass carbon dot shows great potential in bone tissue regeneration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bone regeneration remains a highly dynamic activity during all stages of human growth and development, with a dynamic balance between osteoblasts interacting with each other to produce new bone and osteoclasts resorbing old bone [1]. However, in case of severe damage or disease (e.g., trauma, infection, or arthritis), bone tissue that would otherwise adapt its functional structure to repair itself to the defect may also lose its ability to regenerate itself, so searching for bone implants that can promote bone regeneration has been a pressing issue. Autologous bone, allogeneic bone, decalcified bone matrix, bioceramics, and metallic materials are traditional materials for bone defect repair; however, all of these materials, with the exception of autologous bone with limited bone volume, have the drawbacks of immune rejection and poor biocompatibility [2]. In contrast, with the continuous progress of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have shown even more powerful application prospects in the diagnosis and treatment of major diseases, molecular probes, preparation of 3D nanostructures, slow and controlled release formulations and targeted delivery of drugs [3]. Nanomaterials have been demonstrated to regulate numerous aspects of osteoblast and bone-derived stem cell behavior, including adhesion, migration, and proliferation, making them a useful tool for regenerative medicine [4].

Currently, carbon dots (CDs) are nanostructured materials used in the field of regenerative medicine. CDs are simple to fabricate and have unique properties such as high quantum yield (QY), low toxicity, excellent water solubility, high photostability, etc [5]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) are easily and effectively taken up and modified by cells [6], CDs have been widely used in biomedical and drug delivery systems in recent years [7–9], and a series of new biologically relevant applications have been explored [10,11]. It has been shown that carbon dots can enhance osteogenic differentiation, upregulate osteogenic-related gene (ALP, RUNX2, OCN, BSP) expression and enhance matrix differentiation via the ROS-mediated MAPK pathway [12]. On this basis, Wu et al. synthesized a Ca/P co-doped functionalized CDs that can enter the cytoplasm and increase the expression of osteogenic differentiation genes through endocytosis [13]. However, the current raw materials used for the preparation of carbon dots mostly contain some chemical reagents, which limit their further development in the biomedical field. In recent years, due to the increasing emphasis on the concept of "green chemistry", carbon source precursors for the preparation of carbon dots are mostly used from natural biomass materials, such as soy milk [14], apple juice [15], mangosteen [16], banana peel [17], and wool [18] have been used as carbon sources to prepare fluorescent carbon dots. The use of natural products as carbon sources for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots avoids complicated post-processing procedures and the use of chemical reagents, and is characterized by easy operation, abundant carbon sources and low cost, which is a more studied method at present. In this study, we used lycium ruthenicum as the raw material for the synthesis of carbon dots, which is the fruit with the highest anthocyanin content and rich in many amino acids [19]. However, fewer studies have been reported on the induction of osteoblast differentiation by biomass CDs and the promotion of bone regeneration related applications.

It is generally known that magnesium (Mg), the fourth most common mineral in the human body, plays crucial roles in the expression of several genes, enzyme activity, and cellular processes. Additionally, more than 60% of Mg2+ are found in human bones and teeth. By encouraging the expression of genes associated to osteogenesis and increasing the secretion of hormones or the synthesis of cytokines, Mg2+ can have a major impact on the proliferation and differentiation of osteoblasts [20–22]. There are many reports on the construction of Mg alloys and Mg-doped implants to adhere to osteoblasts and bone regeneration [1,23]. However, magnesium-related materials have insignificant effects on osteoblast differentiation in practical applications, which may be due to the rapid biodegradation of magnesium or the cytotoxicity of high magnesium concentrations [24,25]. Therefore, we considered co-doping magnesium salts with biological carbon dot materials to achieve the synergistic effect of carbon dots and magnesium ions for bone regeneration.

Herein, we synthesized novel fluorescent CDs (Mg-CDs) biomaterials using lycium ruthenicum as the carbon source and doped with different contents of MgCl2. Initially, we determined the carbon source and MgCl2 weight ratio of 1:4 and characterized the morphology, crystal structure and fluorescence properties of the materials by analyzing the UV absorption peaks, surface constituent elements and surface functional groups of Mg-CDs materials doped with different contents of Mg2+, and characterized the morphology, crystal structure and fluorescence properties of the materials. In in-vitro experiments, we co-cultured Mg-CDs with MC3T3 to assess their osteogenesis-promoting ability and determined the optimal dose of Mg-CDs to promote osteoblast differentiation. In order to guarantee Mg-CDs' long-term non-toxicity to cells and tissues after implantation in vivo, we also examined the toxicity of Mg-CDs in vitro. Our study provides a theoretical basis for the application of Mg-doped biogenic carbon dots in bone regeneration in vitro and offers new options for the application of osteogenic nanomaterials.

Lycium ruthenicum were purchased from Anhui Shoufeng Biotechnology Co.; Magnesium chloride hexahydrate was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Founder Reagent Factory. MEM Alpha (α-MEM) medium was purchased from Cytiva (GE Healthcare Life Science, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from CellMax (Gansu, China). 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin, 100 U mL−1 penicillin was purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific (Waltham MA, USA). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) assay kit was purchased from Leagene (Beijing, China). Thiazole blue (MTT) and Alizarin red (ARS) staining solution were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Dexamethasone, L-ascorbic acid and b-glycerophosphate were purchased from Cyagen Biosciences Inc. (Guangzhou, China). Mouse preosteoblast cell line (MC3T3-E1 Subclone 4) was obtained from the laboratory of West China College of Stomatology, Sichuan University (Chengdu, China). The study's reagents were of analytical grade and did not undergo any additional purification. Ultrapure deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Natural lycium ruthenicum were used as the carbon source and magnesium chloride hexahydrate as the metal source to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots by a one-step hydrothermal method. In a classical experiment, 2 g of lycium ruthenicum were added to 80 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined high temperature reaction kettle at 180 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was completed and the kettle was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, the product was filtered using a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane and the resulting CDs solution was obtained. Next, the CDs solution was dialyzed using permeable membrane (MWCO=1 KD) ultrapure water for 24 h, with water changes every 6 h. Finally, the purified carbon point solution was lyophilized in a vacuum lyophilizer to obtain CDs powder. The Mg-CDs were prepared by adding 8 g of magnesium chloride hexahydrate as metal dopant to the reactants in the same experimental procedure. Freeze-drying resulted in the Mg2+-doped biological carbon dots powder. UV–vis spectroscopy was used to identify the absorption peaks of CDs and Mg-CDs, and photoluminescence (PL) and UV–vis absorption spectra were used to describe the materials' fluorescence characteristics. By using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the component elements and surface functional groups of CDs and Mg-CDs were examined. EDS carried out an investigation of the materials' fundamental composition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the dialyzed CDs and Mg-CDs and characterize their appearance and structure. By using X-ray diffraction (XRD), the crystal structures of CDs and Mg-CDs were identified. Zeta-analysis of the surface charges of the materials was performed to assess their stability.

QY= 0.56 quinine sulfate (0.10 mol/L of H2SO4) was used as the PL standard. Quantum yields were calculated as follows.
ɸx=ɸs t (I x/ I s t)(Ƞ2x/ Ƞ2s t)(Ast / Ax)

where ɸx is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area of the fluorescence peak, Ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the absorbance, and s t and x are the standard and test samples, respectively [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytofluorimetric imaging of Mg-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
In contrast to dead cells, which lack this function, exogenous MTT is converted to water-insoluble blue-purple crystals of Formazan by succinate dehydrogenase in the mitochondria of living cells. Therefore, we used MTT assay was used to detect the cytotoxicity of Mg-CDs and the effect on cell proliferation. MC3T3 were inoculated on 96-well plates with 2 × 104 cells per well and incubated in a thermostat for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). After this period, the medium was replaced with 100 µL of α-MEM medium containing different concentrations of Mg-CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 µg mL−1) for another 24 h. (6 replicate wells were set up for each group). Then, 20 µL of MTT culture medium (5 mg mL−1) was added to each culture well and cells were continued to form crystals for 4 h, and the culture media was taken out, 150 L of DMSO was added, and it was agitated for 5 min at room temperature. Using an enzymatic standard and a microplate spectrophotometer, the absorbance (OD) of each well at 490 nm was determined. Cell activity was calculated according to the following equation.
Cell Viability ( % ) =
OD
Treated
OD
Control
× 100 %
ODControl was measured in the absence of Mg-CDs and ODTreated in the presence of Mg-CDs [27].

When MC3T3 were in the logarithmic growth phase, they were counted and 1 × 105 cells were inoculated in a special dish for confocal, and discarded after 24 h of incubation, 200 µg mL−1 Mg-CDs culture solution was added; after 12 h of incubation protected from light, the solution was discarded, the cells in the dish were rinsed 3 times with PBS, 1 mL of 10% formaldehyde was added in the dish, fixed for 15 min, the fixative was discarded, and the cells were carefully washed with The cells in the dishes were carefully rinsed 3 times with PBS, and the cell fluorescence imaging characteristics were observed under different excitation wavelengths by confocal microscopy [27].

MC3T3 were inoculated into 24-well plates at a density of 7 × 104 cells per well and added to complete medium for 24 h. Then osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3 were induced using osteogenic medium and cultured with different concentrations of CDs, Mg-CDs (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 µg mL−1). Throughout the procedure, the osteogenic media, which contained CDs, Mg-CDs, was changed every three days. An alkaline phosphatase kit was used to measure the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) on 3, 7, and 14d following culture. Cells were separated with lysate and then the supernatant was extracted by centrifugation for the assay. The absorbance values of p-nitrophenol at 520 nm were measured on a microplate spectrophotometer using an enzyme standard.

After osteogenesis induction, the mineralized matrix of MC3T3 was examined using alizarin red staining. As stated earlier, cell culture was carried out with a cell inoculation density of 5 × 104 cells per well. On day 21, cells were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min at room temperature and then rinsed three times with deionized water. After that, the cells were stained with 1% alizarin red solution at 37 °C for 30 min, and after that, they were thoroughly washed with deionized water to remove any remaining stain. Finally, the stained cell cultures were checked for calcified nodules using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX51, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR for osteogenic gene expression
ce:section.ce:para: Plate bottom cells were lysed and collected for examination of osteogenic-related gene expression after 7, and 14 days of the previously described induction of osteogenic differentiation. Following the directions in the operating manual, total RNA was isolated by column extraction (M5 Universal RNA Mini Kit, Mei5bio, Beijing). Reverse transcription was used to create first-strand complementary DNA (cDNA) using the M5 Super qPCR RT kit and the gDNA remover synthesis kit (Mei5bio, Beijing). Then, using an RT-qPCR apparatus, qPCR reactions were carried out (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., MA, USA). The 2-Ct technique and -actin as an internal control were used to calculate gene expression. Table 1 contains a list of the PCR amplification primer sequences. All primers were produced by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China).

The mean± standard deviation of all experimental data were calculated from at least three different experiments. SPSS 23.0 software was utilized for the statistical evaluation. It was statistically significant at p < 0.05 [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mg-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method via lycium ruthenicum and magnesium chloride hexahydrate. As shown in Fig. 1d, the Mg-CDs solution showed bright green fluorescence under UV excitation and exhibited excitation-dependent properties of carbon dots (Fig. 1e). FT-IR spectra were used to further analyze the surface functional groups of CDs and Mg-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1a. The CDs and Mg-CDs at 3450–3200 the broad absorption peaks can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the O-H/N-H group [29]. The absorption peaks at 2960 and 1650 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of -CH3 and the stretching vibration of the CO bond in the carboxyl group, respectively [30,31]. The absorption peak at 1400 is attributed to the stretching vibration of -C-N/O [32]. The absorption peaks at 1080 and 676 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-O-C bond and the bending vibration of the C-H bond [30,33]. As can be seen from Fig. 1a, the doping of Mg elements did not affect the types of functional groups on the surface of CDs and the positions of the absorption peaks, maintaining the original functional groups of CDs. Meanwhile, the above results were corroborated with XPS, indicating that the surfaces of CDs and Mg-CDs are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups. These results demonstrate that CDs and Mg-CDs have strong hydrophilicity and water solubility, and the abundant functional groups on the surface make them easy to be functionalized, providing possibilities for future applications. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum gives an absorption peak of Mg-CDs solution at 283 nm, corresponding to the π→π * leap in the CC bond of the carbon nucleus (Fig. 1d) and with an excitation-dependent PL feature (Fig. 1e). It has the maximum fluorescence intensity when the excitation wavelength is chosen to be 427 nm, with an emission peak at 505 nm. Compared to CDs, the surface of Mg-CDs involves a π-π * jump of the CO functional group, and its fluorescence emission peak appears blue-shifted (from 537 nm to 505 nm). Under UV light (λExc=365 nm) CDs emit yellow-green fluorescence, while Mg-CDs exhibit green fluorescence (Fig. 1b), which may be due to the presence of more oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces [34].

Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of X-rays was used to analyze the elemental composition and surface groups of CDs. First, as shown in the full XPS spectrum in Fig. 2a, it can be seen that the CDs are mainly composed of C, O, N, and Mg, with their respective characteristic peaks located at 285.08 eV, 532.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 1298.08 eV. Second, as shown in
Table 2, the content of Mg elements in the CDs increases with the increase of Mg chloride hexahydrate in the reactants from 0.1% to 1.2%. Thus, we successfully achieved the doping of Mg and named the sample with the mass ratio of lycium ruthenicum and MgCl2 hexahydrate as Mg-CDs. Fig. 2b-e show the high-resolution spectra of each element of CDs, and in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (b), three characteristic peaks were recorded at 284.54 eV, 285.63 eV and 288.14 eV, indicating that CDs CO, C-C and C-N bond stretching vibrations are present in them [35]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (c) shows the main peaks of N-H and C-N corresponding to 399.67 eV and 400.75 eV, respectively [35]. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (d), two characteristic peaks are observed at 531.18 eV and 532.26 eV, associated with the vibrations of the O-H and CO groups, respectively [35]. The characteristic peak can be seen in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mg1s (e) located at 1303.74 eV. Fig. 2f-g show the high-resolution spectra of each element of Mg-CDs. Three characteristic peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (f) are located at 284.17 eV, 285.62 eV, 288.48 eV. Two characteristic peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (g) are located at 399.70 eV, 400.87 eV. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s, the two characteristic peaks of (h) in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s are located at 531.40 eV, 532.54 eV. The characteristic peak of (i) in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mg1s is at 1304.13 eV. In summary, the presence of a large number of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CDs and Mg-CDs provides strong evidence for their strong hydrophilicity and high water solubility.

To verify the above XPS elemental content results, EDS was used to analyze the content of each element in CDs and Mg-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3b, the atomic percentage of Mg in the sample Mg-CDs with the mass ratio of reactants lycium ruthenicum and magnesium chloride hexahydrate of 1:4 was as high as 1.2%, which was 12 times higher than that of CDs. In summary, the EDS results and XPS results remain consistent and together verify the successful doping of Mg elements.

The morphology of CDs and Mg-CDs was observed using TEM. As shown in
Fig. 4a, the shape of CDs is roughly spherical with uniform and well-dispersed dimensions, and the diameter range is from 15 nm to 55 nm, with an average diameter of 30.7 ± 0.4 nm and no lattice striations. After doping with magnesium chloride hexahydrate, the diameter range of the obtained Mg-CDs was reduced to 1 nm∼3 nm with an average diameter of 2.0 ± 0.02 nm, with obvious lattice stripes and a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, similar to the reported results in the literature [36]. The X-ray diffractograms (XRD) of the CDs and Mg-CDs are shown in Figure (d). The CDs and Mg-CDs show distinct strong diffraction peaks at 19.37° and 20.31°, respectively, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. In summary, the above results indicate that the prepared CDs and Mg-CDs are graphitized materials, and the doping of Mg ions can improve the crystallinity and further improve the stability of the materials.

As shown in the Fig. 4e, the prepared Mg-CDs have a zeta potential of − 10.6 mv (negative zeta potential) in water, and these surface carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups may result in a number of emission traps in order to achieve high PL characteristics.

The QY of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots we synthesized were 24.3%. Elemental doping modifies the distinctive electrical structure and optical characteristics of CDs and increases the number of active sites on their surface, enhancing their fluorescence qualities [38]. For example, magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg, N-CDs) were synthesized for the determination of paraoxon in the environment with a quantum yield of 33% [39]. The quantum yield of Mg-doped carbon dots used for the detection of arginine was 26.86% [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Mg-CDs on the proliferation of MC3T3
ce:section.ce:para: For Mg-CDs to be used in clinical settings, their biosafety is essential. Although there was no statistically significant difference between the groups (P > 0.05), the findings of the MTT experiment demonstrated that cell viability reduced as the concentration of Mg-CDs in the culture medium rose. This may be due to the higher penetration ability of CDs and more magnesium ions entering the cells in the high concentration Mg-CDs group. High CDs can also cause more oxidative stress, which might result in cell death. The cell survival rate maintained above 80% even when the culture dose of Mg-CDs reached 800 µg mL−1, indicating the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of Mg-CDs. When the culture dose of Mg-CDs was 200 µg mL−1, the cell survival rate was above 85%, indicating that Mg-CDs had no obvious cytotoxicity at low concentrations, indicating their good biocompatibility and suitability for prolonged cell growth, and therefore 200 µg mL−1 was set as a safe concentration for subsequent cell experiments. It is emphasized here that While the cytotoxicity value is lower for the 25 µg mL−1 concentration value, a higher concentration value was chosen as a safe concentration for subsequent studies, considering that the higher concentration value facilitates fluorescence imaging and osteogenic differentiation of cells [27].

To evaluate the cellular internalization of Mg-CDs, i.e., the effect of cellular uptake of Mg-CDs, the confocal imaging results of MC3T3 co-cultured with 200 µg mL−1 Mg-CDs were observed after 12 h. MC3T3 had normal morphology and were irregularly shuttle-shaped. Under the corresponding specific excitation wavelength, different colors of fluorescence could be presented, and the cell shape contour was clearly visible, and the cytoplasm fluorescence intensity was stronger than the nucleus. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was stable, indicating that Mg-CDs could fluorescently label MC3T3. Based on this, Mg-CDs can be used as bioimaging nano reagents to clearly observe the fluorescence within the cytoplasm under fluorescence microscopy with clear cell contours, which will help to visualize the dynamic changes of cell morphology by means of fluorescent imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Mg-CDs on osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3
ce:section.ce:para:
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) assay was used to assess how Mg-CDs affected osteoblast differentiation. ALP is an enzymatic protein secreted by osteoblasts that hydrolyzes organophosphate to induce hydroxyapatite crystal formation, so ALP activity can be used as an indicator for biochemical evaluation of early bone formation. After co-culture of MC3T3 with carbon dots for 3, 7 and 14 d, intracellular ALP increased almost dose-dependently. At the 3rd, 7th and 14th d, the ALP activity expression was considerably elevated when treated with a high concentration of Mg-CDs, but there was no discernible difference between the low concentration group and the control group (P > 0.05) (
Fig. 7a). The results showed that carbon dots promoted the expression of alkaline phosphatase activity in MC3T3 in a dose-dependent manner, and Mg-CDs could act as a good carrier of magnesium ions to affect ALP activity; Mg-CDs significantly increased the ALP activity of the cells and showed a dependence of culture dose and culture time, when the carbon dot concentration was 200 µg mL−1, at 3, 7 and 14d. The expression activity of Mg-CDs was 1.2 times higher than that of the control, indicating that Mg-CDs can promote osteoblast differentiation. It has been noted that Mg2+ has a role in affecting osteoblast proliferation and differentiation [40,41], and we suggest that CDs, as an easily accessible and biocompatible nanocarrier, can effectively bring and act on Mg2+ into cells. To illustrate the role of CDs carriers and Mg2+ doping in osteogenesis, parallel experiments were performed with CDs and Mg-CDs as two control groups. As can be seen, when the culture concentration and time were the same (200 µg mL−1; 3, 7 days), the CDs group had little effect on the ALP activity of MC3T3, however, the effect was more pronounced in the Mg-CDs group, suggesting that it is Mg2+ that plays a role in inducing osteogenesis. In conclusion, Mg2+ doping and the efficient carrier provided by CDs together determine the excellent osteogenic activity of Mg-CDs, and there may be other mechanisms by which Mg-CDs affect alkaline phosphatase activity. In this study we used MC3T3-E1 Subclone 4, which has little overlap with the gene expression profile of primary cranial osteoblasts and is capable of exhibiting high levels of osteogenic differentiation in growth and will form a well mineralized extracellular matrix (ECM) [42].

The development and mineralization of the bone matrix are regarded as important indicators of osteogenic differentiation. MC3T3 produced calcium deposition in the extracellular matrix was found using alizarin red staining (ARS). As shown in the figure, positive ARS staining was found in all groups. a dose-dependent increase in calcified nodules was observed in the Mg-CDs group. The ARS displayed an increase in calcium nodules and a considerable increase in osteoblast mineralization when the concentration of Mg-CDs was between 100 and 200 µg mL−1. This may be due to the fact that nano-Mg-CDs promoted the uptake of Mg2+ by the cells through the endocytic pathway, thus allowing intracellular release of Mg2+. The cell mineralization results again demonstrated that Mg-CDs can effectively promote osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3 [27]. The addition of Mg-CDs resulted in an upregulation of osteogenesis. However, the underlying mechanisms still need to be further investigated.

Osteogenesis-related genes, including as ALP, Runx-2, Col-I, and OC, were considerably elevated in the Mg-CDs group compared to controls and upregulated in the CDs group as well ( Fig. 9a-d). Varied stages of a person's development result in different expression patterns of osteoblast-specific genes. In osteoblastic developed cells with mineralized matrix, ALP is primarily generated and expressed as an early hallmark of osteogenic differentiation [43]. Runx-2 is a crucial transcription factor for osteogenesis that controls bone development, osteoblast differentiation, and extracellular matrix maintenance [44]. The primary component of osteoblast extracellular matrix deposition is Col-I [45]. OC appears at the start of matrix deposition and is a hallmark of late osteoblast development [46]. It has not been suggested to use a conventional osteogenic gene expression threshold to determine if a substance encourages osteogenesis. However, orthopedic biomaterials are typically classified as "osteogenic" when ALP, Runx2, Col-I, and OC expression levels are 1.5 times higher than the control value [47,48]. Our research demonstrated that, in comparison to the pure CDs group, MC3T3 in the Mg-CDs group expressed higher levels of ALP, Runx2, Col-I, and OC. In conclusion, Mg-CDs effectively promote osteogenesis by upregulating intracellular osteogenesis-related mRNA expression and can be used as novel osteogenic nanomaterials.

In addition, Yang et al. synthesized a Mg-CDs from magnesium gluconate [27] and characterized its physicochemical properties, in vitro cytotoxicity, fluorescence imaging and osteogenic differentiation assays, and a comparison of the two Mg-CDs is shown in chart.

A complex physiological process called bone repair occurs when damaged bone tissue is replaced with new bone [49]. Stem cells have a propensity to differentiate into osteoblasts during this process and secrete a variety of bone matrix proteins in the direction of the damaged site. After mineral deposition, new bone is created to fill the gap in the bone tissue [50]. Using a one-step pyrolysis technique, we created Mg-doped biogenic carbon dots (Mg-CDs) with lycium ruthenicum origin for the investigation. Our research revealed that Mg-CDs had good in vitro osteogenic activity. In addition to costly cytokines like BMP2, Mg-CDs, a new bioactive nanomaterial, offer another option for bone regeneration.

Our study shows that the functionalized Mg-CDs synthesized from lycium ruthenicum and doped with Mg2+ had high biocompatibility and the magnesium content is an important factor affecting the osteogenic capacity. Our work determined that the optimal ratio of carbon source to magnesium source for Mg-doped carbon dot preparation is 1:4 and that Mg-CD containing 200 µg mL−1 is the optimal concentration for the proliferation and differentiation of osteoblasts in in vitro culture. It is significant that we discovered that Mg-CDs can successfully enhance bone regeneration in vitro. However, more research is required to clarify the precise mechanisms underlying the ability of Mg-CDs to regenerate bone. Our research ingeniously hypothesizes potential uses for Mg-CDs in bone disorders and tissue regeneration.

This research was funded by Graduate Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2021Y431), Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province (202102130501002) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022–120).
Huaiyi Cheng: Data curation. Yue Wang: Visualization. Qingmei Zhang: Supervision. Lupeng Zhang: Supervision. Lu Wang: Supervision. Ran Li: Supervision. Xiuping Wu: Validation. Bing Li: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
